content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 15, 1994

Gary J. Grimm
California Regional Water Quality Control Board
San Francisco Bay Region
2101 Webster Street, Suite 500
Oakland, CA  94612

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-206

Dear Mr. Grimm:

You have requested advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You are
employed as a staff counsel for the State Water Resources Control
Board (state board) and provide legal services and advice to the
California Regional Water Quality Control Board, San Francisco Bay
Region (regional board).

QUESTION

May you provide legal advice or participate in decisions of
the regional board concerning matters relating to Stanford
University since your stepson will receive a financial aid offer
from Stanford University?

CONCLUSION

Since your stepson is over 18 years of age, he is not
considered a dependent child for purposes of the Act. Therefore,
his financial aid offer from Stanford University does not prohibit
you from participating in matters relating to Stanford University.

FACTS

As staff counsel, you advise the regional board staff and
members on legal matters relating to the board's regulatory
activities.  The regional board's water quality regulatory
activities include issuance of waste discharge requirements and
appropriate enforcement actions to persons discharging waste

1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-94-206
Page 2


within the region.  Several sites owned or operated by Stanford
University are the subject of these regulations.  On July 20,
1994, the regional board will hear and decide several matters
relating to Stanford University.

Your stepson, John Peck, will be a freshman at Stanford
University in the fall of 1994.  John has accepted a financial aid
offer of approximately $17,000.  The financial aid package includes
a scholarship, job eligibility and a federal Perkins loan.  John
is 18 years old and is financially dependent on you and your wife.

ANALYSIS


     Section 87103 prohibits a public official from participating
in a decision which will have a material financial effect on "a
member of his or her immediate family."  Section 82029 defines
"immediate family" as the spouse and dependent children.

     Since the term "dependent child" is not defined by the
statute, we have previously advised that a dependent child of a
public official is a child who is under 18 years of age and who
the official is eligible to claim as a dependent for federal
income tax purposes.  (Tremlett Advice Letter, No. I-89-386, copy
enclosed.)  We are currently in the process of codifying this
definition by proposed Regulation 18229, copy enclosed.²

     Under proposed Regulation 18229, an official's child who is
over 18 years of age is not considered a dependent child for
purposes of the Act.³  Therefore, you may participate in decisions
regarding Stanford University if you have no other economic
interests which will be materially affected.




     ² The Commission is scheduled to adopt this regulation in
September 1994.  Additional proposed language to define a
"dependent child" includes the child of the spouse of a public
official.  In any case, since John is 18 years old, he is not
considered a dependent child under either our current advice or
the proposed regulation.

     ³ Under the proposed regulation, a child over 18 years of age is
not considered a dependent child.  Although it is not likely that
the Commission will significantly deviate from this proposal, the
Commission has not formally adopted this regulation and it is
possible that our advice could change.  Please consult the adopted
regulation after September 1, 1994.

File No. A-94-206
Page 3


I trust this answers your question.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                   By: Jill Stscher
                                       Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosures

QUESTION: May you provide legal advice or participate in decisions of
the regional board concerning matters relating to Stanford
University since your stepson will receive a financial aid offer
from Stanford University?

CONCLUSION: Since your stepson is over 18 years of age,
he is not
considered a dependent child for purposes of the Act.
Therefore,
his financial aid offer from Stanford University does not prohibit
you from participating in matters relating to Stanford University.
